title: The Springfield Three — Vanished Without a Trace
description: On June 7, 1992, three women — Sherrill Levitt, her daughter Suzie Streeter, and Suzie's friend Stacy McCall — vanished overnight from a Springfield, Missouri home. No blood, no signs of struggle, no bodies, no suspects. Thirty years later, it remains one of America's most baffling missing persons cases.

segments.voice: Springfield, Missouri. June 6 to 7, 1992. Three women are at a modest home on East Delmar Street. Sherrill Levitt, 47, lives there with her daughter Suzie Streeter, 19. That night, Suzie's friend Stacy McCall, also 19, stays over after their high school graduation party. It is a Saturday night. In the morning, all three women are gone — along with their cars, their purses, and apparently their very existence. No note. No sign of a struggle. No trace.
segments.visual: Springfield Missouri suburban home 1992 summer night

segments.voice: Friends who came to pick up Stacy and Suzie the next day noticed the front door was ajar. The light was on. The television was on. The women's cars were still parked outside — which immediately eliminated the idea that they had left willingly. Inside, the breakfast dishes were still there. Cigarettes were still burning in an ashtray. Everything suggested the women had simply... stopped.
segments.visual: house door ajar light on cigarette ashtray abandoned

segments.voice: The only sign of anything unusual was a broken glass globe on the porch light. It appeared to have been knocked off, perhaps during some kind of altercation outside. But there was no blood, no weapons, no physical evidence of violence. The absence of evidence was, paradoxically, the most disturbing evidence of all. Someone had taken three women from a home on a residential street without leaving any forensic trace.
segments.visual: broken porch light globe only clue evidence

segments.voice: Police initially treated the case cautiously — wondering if the women had voluntarily disappeared together for some reason. But this theory collapsed quickly. Stacy and Suzie barely knew each other well enough to plan something together. Sherrill had a job, a home, a life she was embedded in. There was no financial crisis, no relationship drama, no reason to run. The women had simply vanished.
segments.visual: investigation theory collapse no motive no flight

segments.voice: The timing added another layer of pain. June 7, 1992 was the same day Stacy and Suzie graduated from Kickapoo High School. Their families and friends were waiting for them to appear at celebrations. When they didn't show, the calls started. The discovery that they were missing came on what should have been one of the happiest days of their young lives.
segments.visual: high school graduation day celebration missing family

segments.voice: The investigation in 1992 was limited by the forensic tools of the time. No DNA testing on the scene. No digital records, no cell phone data, no security cameras. Neighbors were interviewed and reported nothing unusual. A car that may have been parked near the house overnight was mentioned by one witness but never traced. The physical evidence was essentially zero.

Over the years, investigators developed a working theory: the perpetrator or perpetrators were familiar to at least one of the women, had a way of gaining entry without force, and had the means and capability to remove three adults from the premises without leaving any physical trace and without attracting notice. This suggested someone either trusted by the women, or a sophisticated criminal actor — or both.
segments.visual: suspect theory trusted person entry no force

segments.voice: The case generated a significant number of tips and persons of interest over the years. One man — Robert Craig Cox, a convicted kidnapper — told a prison associate that he knew where three bodies were in Springfield and that they would never be found. Cox was interviewed multiple times by investigators. He eventually gave a statement denying involvement but maintaining his cryptic claim. He has never been charged.
segments.visual: Robert Craig Cox person of interest kidnapper interview

segments.voice: Cox's statement to his prison associate became the most chilling lead in the case. He described the women's bodies as being in a place 'you'll never find them.' Whether this reflected genuine knowledge or a manipulative claim designed to create infamy for himself is debated. Investigators took it seriously enough to pursue multiple searches. None turned up remains.
segments.visual: prison statement bodies location never found chilling

segments.voice: The families of the three women have kept the case in public consciousness through decades of advocacy. Sherill Levitt's mother Lorene Craig died without knowing what happened to her daughter. Stacy McCall's parents formed an advocacy group. Suzie Streeter's family has given repeated interviews. The grief of parents and grandparents who outlive their children, without knowing where those children are, is a particular and relentless kind of suffering.
segments.visual: family advocacy grief decades searching memorial

segments.voice: The Springfield Three case has attracted attention from every generation of true crime investigators. Online amateur sleuths have developed elaborate theories. Professional investigators have revisited the case multiple times. The Missouri State Highway Patrol maintains it as an active investigation. As recently as 2021, investigators conducted new searches at specific properties based on new information. No remains were found.
segments.visual: online investigation sleuths amateur theories property search

segments.voice: What makes this case uniquely haunting is the complete absence of the usual forensic narrative. In most missing persons cases, there is a last known location, a movement pattern, a trail that eventually leads somewhere. The Springfield Three simply stopped existing at some point on the night of June 6th or the morning of June 7th. The absence of any trace is not just a forensic mystery — it is almost metaphysically disturbing.
segments.visual: absence evidence metaphysical mystery no trace

segments.voice: Stacy McCall would be 51 years old today. Suzie Streeter would also be 51. Sherrill Levitt would be 79. Three women who should have had full lives — Suzie and Stacy just beginning theirs, Sherrill in the middle of hers. They were gone before the sun came up on June 7, 1992. Somewhere, someone knows where they are. Somewhere, the answer to one of America's most baffling disappearances is waiting to be found.
segments.visual: graduated portraits young women ages today memorial

segments.voice: If you have any information about the disappearance of Sherrill Levitt, Suzie Streeter, or Stacy McCall, the Springfield Police Department maintains an active tip line. Cold cases are solved. Evidence degrades but sometimes also preserves. Witnesses develop consciences over time. The Springfield Three have waited 30 years for someone who knows the truth to speak it. Their families are still waiting.
segments.visual: tip line cold case solved hope families waiting
